description: Full Transformer model architecture diagram showing the encoder stack (left) with multi-head attention, feed-forward layers, and positional encoding, and the decoder stack (right) with masked multi-head attention, encoder-decoder attention, and feed-forward layers, connected by residual connections and layer normalization.
teaching_value: Gives students a complete visual map of how encoder and decoder components connect, making it easier to understand data flow, residual connections, and the role of each sub-layer before diving into individual components.
when_to_show: Show at the start of any lesson on the Transformer to orient students to the overall architecture before explaining individual components in detail.
suggested_caption: The Transformer encoder-decoder architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Scaled Dot-Product Attention diagram showing the computation flow: inputs Q, K, V → MatMul(Q,K) → Scale by 1/sqrt(d_k) → optional Mask → Softmax → MatMul with V → output.
teaching_value: Illustrates step-by-step how queries, keys, and values are combined to produce attention-weighted outputs, helping students understand the mathematical operations and their order before seeing the formula.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the attention mechanism formula; use alongside the equation Attention(Q,K,V)=softmax(QK^T/sqrt(d_k))V to connect math to computation graph.
suggested_caption: Scaled Dot-Product Attention computation flow (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Multi-Head Attention diagram showing h parallel scaled dot-product attention heads receiving linearly projected Q, K, V inputs, whose outputs are concatenated and linearly projected to produce the final output.
teaching_value: Shows students how multiple attention heads operate in parallel on different learned projections, clarifying why multi-head attention can capture diverse relationships compared to single-head attention.
when_to_show: Show after explaining single-head scaled dot-product attention to illustrate how multi-head attention extends it by running h attention functions simultaneously.
suggested_caption: Multi-Head Attention with h parallel attention layers (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Attention visualization showing colored lines connecting the word 'making' in an input sentence to other words across the sequence, with different colors representing different attention heads in encoder self-attention layer 5, highlighting long-distance dependency capture for the phrase 'making…more difficult'.
teaching_value: Provides concrete evidence that attention heads learn linguistically meaningful long-range dependencies, helping students understand self-attention as more than a mathematical abstraction.
when_to_show: Show when discussing what self-attention learns in practice, especially to motivate why attention is preferable to RNNs for capturing long-range dependencies.
suggested_caption: Encoder self-attention capturing long-distance verb dependencies (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Attention visualization (top panel) showing full attention patterns for head 5 in layer 5, with lines connecting all words in a sentence, illustrating broad attention distribution relevant to anaphora resolution.
teaching_value: Demonstrates that individual attention heads develop specialized roles such as coreference/anaphora resolution, helping students understand the division of labor across heads.
when_to_show: Show when teaching about emergent linguistic structure in attention heads, particularly to illustrate how different heads learn different syntactic/semantic tasks.
suggested_caption: Full attention pattern for head 5 showing anaphora resolution (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Attention visualization (bottom panel) showing isolated attention from the word 'its' for attention heads 5 and 6 in layer 5, with very sharp (concentrated) attention lines pointing to the antecedent noun, illustrating precise anaphora resolution.
teaching_value: Shows students that attention can be highly focused for specific words, and that different heads (5 vs 6) may resolve the same coreference with different sharpness, deepening understanding of head diversity.
when_to_show: Show alongside the full-attention panel (Image 5) to contrast broad vs. sharp attention patterns and reinforce the concept of head specialization.
suggested_caption: Sharp isolated attention from 'its' resolving anaphora (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Attention visualization from one encoder self-attention head at layer 5 showing attention patterns across a full sentence, with colored lines suggesting the head has learned sentence-structure-related behavior such as syntactic dependencies.
teaching_value: Provides visual evidence that attention heads implicitly learn syntactic structure without explicit supervision, helping students appreciate the representational power of self-attention.
when_to_show: Show when discussing what Transformers learn implicitly, especially in lessons comparing Transformers to syntax-aware models or discussing interpretability.
suggested_caption: Encoder self-attention head learning sentence structure (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Attention visualization from a second encoder self-attention head at layer 5 showing a different attention pattern across the same or similar sentence, contrasting with the first head to illustrate that different heads learn different tasks.
teaching_value: Directly demonstrates task specialization across attention heads by showing two heads with visually distinct patterns, reinforcing why multi-head attention is more powerful than single-head attention.
when_to_show: Show paired with Image 7 to highlight the contrast between two heads and reinforce the concept that multi-head attention captures diverse linguistic relationships simultaneously.
suggested_caption: Second encoder self-attention head with distinct learned behavior (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: A flow diagram (Fig. 11.3.1) showing how attention pooling computes a weighted average of values. It illustrates the pipeline: queries and keys feed into an attention scoring function 'a', the scores pass through softmax to produce attention weights, which then weight the values to produce the output.
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete visual of the full attention computation pipeline — from raw scores through softmax normalization to the final weighted value aggregation — making the abstract formula tangible.
when_to_show: Show at the start of any lesson on attention mechanisms, before introducing specific scoring functions like dot-product or additive attention, to establish the shared computational framework.
suggested_caption: Attention pooling: scoring function + softmax yields weighted value average. (D2L.ai)

description: A code output visualization (likely a heatmap or tensor printout) showing the result of applying DotProductAttention to a toy minibatch with batch size 2, 10 keys/values of dimension 4, and valid lengths 2 and 6. Expected output shape is 2×1×4.
teaching_value: Lets students verify their understanding of scaled dot-product attention by seeing the actual numerical output, reinforcing how valid lengths mask out irrelevant keys and how the output tensor shape is determined.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the DotProductAttention implementation and before students run the code themselves, to set expectations for what correct output looks like.
suggested_caption: DotProductAttention output: 2×1×4 tensor for minibatch size 2. (D2L.ai)

description: A second code output or visualization from the DotProductAttention toy example, likely showing attention weights or an intermediate result (e.g., softmax scores) for the same minibatch setup with valid lengths 2 and 6.
teaching_value: Provides an additional view of the scaled dot-product attention computation, helping students trace how masking and softmax interact to produce sparse or concentrated attention distributions.
when_to_show: Show alongside image 2 when walking through the DotProductAttention example step by step, to illustrate intermediate attention weight distributions.
suggested_caption: Attention weights from DotProductAttention with valid-length masking. (D2L.ai)

description: A third output visualization from the DotProductAttention toy example, likely a heatmap of attention weights across the 10 keys for each of the 2 batch examples, showing how valid lengths 2 and 6 concentrate attention on valid positions.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the effect of valid-length masking: attention is forced to zero on padded positions, helping students understand why masking is critical for variable-length sequences.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how valid lengths prevent the model from attending to padded positions, using the contrast between the two batch examples (valid lengths 2 vs. 6) as a teaching moment.
suggested_caption: Heatmap of masked attention weights: valid lengths 2 vs. 6. (D2L.ai)

description: A fourth output visualization from the DotProductAttention toy example, likely showing the final output tensor values or a summary plot confirming the 2×1×4 output shape after weighted value aggregation.
teaching_value: Completes the walkthrough of DotProductAttention by showing the final aggregated output, reinforcing how attention weights combine values into a single context vector per query.
when_to_show: Show as the concluding step of the DotProductAttention example to tie together scoring, masking, softmax, and aggregation into one complete forward pass.
suggested_caption: Final DotProductAttention output: weighted value aggregation result. (D2L.ai)

description: A code output or visualization from the AdditiveAttention toy example with queries of shape (2,1,20), keys (2,10,2), values (2,10,4), and valid lengths (2,6). Likely shows the output tensor or attention scores for the first step of the additive attention computation.
teaching_value: Helps students see how additive attention handles queries and keys of different dimensionalities (20-dim queries vs. 2-dim keys) by projecting them into a shared hidden space, contrasting with dot-product attention.
when_to_show: Show when introducing additive attention and contrasting it with dot-product attention, emphasizing that it works when query and key dimensions differ.
suggested_caption: AdditiveAttention output for 20-dim queries and 2-dim keys. (D2L.ai)

description: A second output visualization from the AdditiveAttention toy example, likely showing attention weights or intermediate MLP activations for the same (2,1,20)/(2,10,2)/(2,10,4) setup with valid lengths (2,6).
teaching_value: Illustrates how the tanh-activated MLP in additive attention produces attention scores, helping students connect the mathematical formula (W_q*q + W_k*k passed through tanh then w_v) to actual computed values.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the internal MLP computation of additive attention, to ground the abstract weight matrices W_q, W_k, w_v in a concrete numerical example.
suggested_caption: AdditiveAttention intermediate scores via tanh MLP. (D2L.ai)

description: A third output visualization from the AdditiveAttention toy example, likely a heatmap of softmax attention weights showing how valid-length masking affects the distribution across 10 keys for each of the 2 batch examples.
teaching_value: Allows direct visual comparison with the DotProductAttention heatmap (image 4), showing that both methods produce similar masking behavior despite different scoring mechanisms, reinforcing the role of softmax and masking.
when_to_show: Show side-by-side with the DotProductAttention heatmap to help students compare how the two scoring functions produce different raw scores but similar masked attention patterns.
suggested_caption: AdditiveAttention weight heatmap with valid-length masking applied. (D2L.ai)

description: A fourth output visualization from the AdditiveAttention toy example, likely showing the final 2×1×4 output tensor after weighted value aggregation, completing the additive attention forward pass demonstration.
teaching_value: Closes the additive attention example by showing the final context vectors, allowing students to compare the numerical outputs of additive vs. dot-product attention on the same value set and understand that both produce the same output shape.
when_to_show: Show as the final step of the AdditiveAttention walkthrough, then prompt students to compare this output with the DotProductAttention output to discuss similarities and differences.
suggested_caption: AdditiveAttention final output: 2×1×4 context vectors produced. (D2L.ai)

description: Architecture diagram of a bidirectional RNN showing two hidden layer sequences: a forward hidden state (H with right arrow) processing inputs left-to-right and a backward hidden state (H with left arrow) processing inputs right-to-left, with both concatenated to produce outputs at each time step.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates how bidirectional RNNs simultaneously capture past (forward) and future (backward) context at each time step, making it concrete why filling in 'I am ___ hungry' benefits from seeing both preceding and following words.
when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing bidirectional RNNs, especially after presenting the fill-in-the-blank motivation example, to bridge the intuition of needing both past and future context with the actual architectural mechanism.
suggested_caption: Bidirectional RNN: forward and backward hidden states combined at each step. (D2L.ai)

description: A compact, rolled-up diagram of a recurrent neural network showing a single neural network module A with input x_t, output h_t, and a self-loop arrow indicating recurrent connection
teaching_value: Introduces the concept of recurrence visually — the loop makes explicit that hidden state is fed back into the network, helping students grasp why RNNs can 'remember' without yet seeing the unrolled form
when_to_show: Show first when introducing RNNs, before unrolling — ask students what the loop represents and what information it carries
suggested_caption: Rolled RNN: module A recurrently passes hidden state h_t. (Olah, 2015)

description: An unrolled recurrent neural network showing multiple copies of module A across time steps t-1, t, t+1, each receiving input x_t and producing output h_t, with arrows connecting hidden states sequentially
teaching_value: Makes the temporal unfolding of an RNN concrete — students can see how the same weights are reused at each time step and how information flows from past to present, clarifying the chain-like structure
when_to_show: Show immediately after the rolled RNN diagram to demonstrate that the loop is equivalent to a chain; useful when explaining backpropagation through time
suggested_caption: Unrolled RNN: identical modules share weights across time steps. (Olah, 2015)

description: An unrolled RNN diagram illustrating short-term dependencies, where the relevant prior context (shown in darker shading) is only a few steps away from the current prediction point
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the easy case for RNNs — when relevant context is nearby, gradient signals remain strong and the network can learn the dependency, setting up contrast with the long-term case
when_to_show: Show when introducing the long-term dependency problem; use as the 'easy case' before showing the long-term dependencies diagram to build contrast
suggested_caption: Short-term dependencies: nearby context is easily captured by standard RNNs. (Olah, 2015)

alt: Neural networks struggle with long term dependencies.
description: An unrolled RNN diagram illustrating long-term dependencies, where the relevant prior context is many time steps away from the current prediction, with fading or distant connections indicating difficulty
teaching_value: Visually motivates the need for LSTMs by showing that when relevant context is far away, standard RNNs struggle — the gap between information and where it is needed makes learning difficult due to vanishing gradients
when_to_show: Show after the short-term dependencies diagram; ask students why the distance between context and prediction matters before revealing the LSTM solution
suggested_caption: Long-term dependencies: distant context is hard for standard RNNs to learn. (Olah, 2015)

description: An unrolled standard RNN repeating module showing a single tanh layer inside each time-step block, with input x_t, hidden state h_t-1, and output h_t
teaching_value: Establishes the baseline architecture students need to contrast with LSTMs — the simplicity of a single tanh layer makes the complexity and power of the LSTM's four interacting layers more meaningful
when_to_show: Show just before introducing the LSTM chain diagram; explicitly ask students to note how many layers are inside the module before switching to the LSTM version
suggested_caption: Standard RNN repeating module: a single tanh layer per time step. (Olah, 2015)

description: An unrolled LSTM chain diagram showing repeating modules with four interacting neural network layers (yellow boxes), pointwise operations (pink circles), and both cell state and hidden state lines running across time steps
teaching_value: Provides the full LSTM architecture overview — students can see the increased complexity over simple RNNs and begin to identify the cell state highway and gate structures before the step-by-step walkthrough
when_to_show: Show after the simple RNN module; use as a 'big picture' before walking through each gate — ask students to identify what is new compared to the simple RNN
suggested_caption: LSTM chain: four interacting layers per module enable long-term memory. (Olah, 2015)

description: A notation legend diagram for LSTM diagrams showing: yellow boxes (neural network layers), pink circles (pointwise operations), single arrows (vector flow), merging lines (concatenation), and forking lines (copy operations)
teaching_value: Provides the visual vocabulary needed to read all subsequent LSTM diagrams — without understanding this notation, students cannot interpret the gate diagrams; explicitly teaching notation prevents confusion
when_to_show: Show before any detailed LSTM gate diagram; return to it whenever a student seems confused by a symbol in a subsequent diagram
suggested_caption: LSTM notation guide: boxes, circles, and lines explained. (Olah, 2015)

description: An LSTM diagram highlighting the cell state as a horizontal line running across the top of the network through all time steps, with minimal interactions shown, emphasizing its role as an information highway
teaching_value: Isolates the most important concept in LSTMs — the cell state conveyor belt — helping students understand how information can flow unchanged across many time steps, which is the core solution to vanishing gradients
when_to_show: Show when first explaining the cell state concept; use the conveyor belt analogy and ask students what advantage a direct path for information provides over the hidden state alone
suggested_caption: LSTM cell state: an information highway enabling long-range memory. (Olah, 2015)

description: A diagram of a single LSTM gate showing a sigmoid neural network layer feeding into a pointwise multiplication operation, illustrating how gates control information flow with outputs between 0 and 1
teaching_value: Explains the fundamental gating mechanism used three times in an LSTM — students learn that sigmoid outputs act as soft switches (0=block, 1=pass), which is the building block for understanding forget, input, and output gates
when_to_show: Show before introducing any specific gate (forget/input/output); ensure students can explain what a value of 0 vs 1 means before proceeding to the full LSTM walkthrough
suggested_caption: LSTM gate: sigmoid layer controls how much information passes through. (Olah, 2015)

description: An LSTM diagram with the forget gate highlighted, showing a sigmoid layer taking h_t-1 and x_t as inputs and producing f_t, which is multiplied pointwise with the previous cell state C_t-1
teaching_value: Teaches the first of three LSTM gates — students see concretely how the network learns to selectively erase information from the cell state, with the language modeling example (forgetting subject gender) making it intuitive
when_to_show: Show as the first step in the LSTM walkthrough after explaining the gate notation; use the 'predicting next word after subject changes' example to ground the abstract mechanism
suggested_caption: Forget gate: sigmoid decides what to erase from cell state C_{t-1}. (Olah, 2015)

description: LSTM diagram highlighting the input gate: a sigmoid layer (input gate) and a tanh layer producing candidate values C̃_t, showing how new information is prepared for storage in the cell state.
teaching_value: Isolates the input gate mechanism so students can focus on how the network decides what new information to write into memory, separating it from the forget and output steps.
when_to_show: After explaining the forget gate; use this to show the second of three LSTM gate operations before combining them in the cell state update.
suggested_caption: LSTM input gate: sigmoid selects updates, tanh creates candidates C̃_t. (Olah, 2015)

description: LSTM diagram highlighting the cell state update: the old cell state C_{t-1} is multiplied by the forget gate f_t, then added to i_t * C̃_t, producing the new cell state C_t.
teaching_value: Shows the core memory update arithmetic in one focused view, making explicit how forgetting and writing interact via element-wise multiplication and addition on the cell state highway.
when_to_show: After introducing both the forget gate and input gate; use this as the synthesis step showing how both gates jointly modify the cell state.
suggested_caption: LSTM cell state update: forget old info, add new candidate values. (Olah, 2015)

description: LSTM diagram highlighting the output gate: a sigmoid layer filters which parts of the cell state to output, the cell state is passed through tanh, and the two are multiplied to produce h_t.
teaching_value: Clarifies that the hidden state output is a filtered, bounded version of the cell state, helping students understand why the network doesn't simply expose raw memory to downstream layers.
when_to_show: After the cell state update step; use this to complete the full LSTM forward pass and explain what gets passed to the next time step and to downstream layers.
suggested_caption: LSTM output gate: sigmoid filters cell state through tanh to produce h_t. (Olah, 2015)

description: LSTM variant diagram showing peephole connections, where additional lines feed the cell state C_t directly into all three gate sigmoid layers, allowing gates to condition on the current memory value.
teaching_value: Demonstrates a concrete architectural modification to standard LSTMs, helping students understand how peepholes give gates direct access to cell state magnitude, not just the hidden state.
when_to_show: After fully explaining the standard LSTM; introduce when discussing architectural variants and why researchers modify the baseline model.
suggested_caption: Peephole LSTM: gate layers receive direct cell state input. (Olah, 2015)

description: LSTM variant diagram showing coupled forget and input gates, where a single decision controls both forgetting and inputting: the input gate is replaced by (1 - f_t), so the network only writes when it forgets.
teaching_value: Illustrates how reducing independent gate decisions simplifies the model and enforces a conservation constraint on memory, helping students reason about design trade-offs in LSTM variants.
when_to_show: When comparing LSTM variants; use alongside the peephole diagram to show a spectrum of modifications from additive (peepholes) to reductive (coupled gates).
suggested_caption: Coupled-gate LSTM: forget and input decisions are tied together. (Olah, 2015)

alt: A gated recurrent unit neural network.
description: Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) architecture diagram showing a reset gate and update gate, with merged cell and hidden state, illustrating a simpler two-gate recurrent unit compared to the standard LSTM.
teaching_value: Provides a visual contrast to the full LSTM, helping students understand how the GRU achieves similar long-term dependency modeling with fewer parameters and a unified state vector.
when_to_show: After covering standard LSTM and its variants; use as a capstone comparison to show how architectural choices affect model complexity and state representation.
suggested_caption: GRU: two gates and merged cell/hidden state simplify the LSTM. (Olah, 2015)

description: Diagram showing the context vector in a seq2seq encoder-decoder architecture, visualizing the context as a fixed-size vector (size 4 shown) produced by the encoder RNN and passed to the decoder RNN, with hidden states illustrated at each time step.
teaching_value: Concretely shows students what the 'context vector' is — a fixed-size numerical array — and how it serves as the sole information bridge between encoder and decoder, making the bottleneck problem intuitive.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the encoder-decoder bottleneck problem, right before explaining why attention was needed; helps students visualize what information is compressed into a single vector.
suggested_caption: Context vector (size 4) bridges encoder and decoder in seq2seq. — Jay Alammar

description: Diagram illustrating word embeddings transforming discrete words into dense vectors, and showing how an RNN processes sequential inputs by combining an input embedding vector with the previous hidden state at each time step.
teaching_value: Establishes the visual language used throughout the article — showing how words become vectors and how hidden states are updated step-by-step — giving students a concrete mental model of RNN mechanics before attention is introduced.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how words are fed into an RNN encoder; use before discussing hidden states or attention to ensure students understand the input representation pipeline.
suggested_caption: Word embeddings feed into RNN steps, updating hidden states sequentially. — Jay Alammar

description: Diagram contrasting classic seq2seq with an attention-based model, showing that the encoder passes ALL hidden states (not just the last one) to the decoder, illustrating the key architectural difference that enables attention.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the core architectural change in attention models — passing all encoder hidden states instead of one — helping students understand why attention solves the bottleneck and how the decoder gains access to richer context.
when_to_show: Show at the moment of introducing attention as a solution to the context vector bottleneck; contrast directly with the previous context vector diagram to highlight what changes architecturally.
suggested_caption: Attention: encoder passes all hidden states to decoder, not just the last. — Jay Alammar

description: Visualization of attention weights across input sentence words at each decoding step, showing which source words the model focuses on when generating each target word, likely as a heatmap or highlighted sentence alignment for a French-to-English translation example.
teaching_value: Makes the abstract concept of 'attention' concrete by showing actual word-level alignment patterns, demonstrating that the model learns non-trivial, non-monotonic alignments (e.g., French-English word order differences) from training data.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the attention mechanism mathematically or architecturally; use to demonstrate real-world learned alignment behavior and to motivate why attention improves translation of long or reordered sentences.
suggested_caption: Attention weights reveal which input words inform each decoded output word. — Jay Alammar

description: Architecture diagram of a sequence-to-sequence model showing an RNN encoder processing input tokens (e.g., 'They', 'are', 'watching') into a fixed-shape context vector, which initializes an RNN decoder that generates output tokens (e.g., 'Ils', 'regardent') one at a time, with special BOS/EOS tokens marking sequence boundaries.
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete visual of how variable-length input sequences are compressed into a fixed context vector and how the decoder autoregressively generates output, making the information bottleneck and sequential generation process intuitive.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of introducing seq2seq models, before any code, to anchor the overall data flow from source to target sequence.
suggested_caption: Seq2seq RNN encoder–decoder for machine translation. (D2L.ai Fig. 10.7.1)

description: Detailed diagram of the seq2seq decoder showing how the encoder's final hidden state initializes the decoder, and how the context variable is concatenated with the decoder input at every time step before being fed into the RNN layers, with a fully connected output layer on top.
teaching_value: Clarifies the two key design choices in the decoder: (1) using the encoder's final hidden state as the initial decoder state, and (2) concatenating the context vector with every decoder input — helping students understand why these choices propagate source information throughout decoding.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the decoder implementation, specifically when discussing how encoded information is passed to and maintained throughout the decoder.
suggested_caption: Decoder detail: context vector concatenated at each time step. (D2L.ai)

description: Training loss curve (likely loss vs. epoch or iteration) for the seq2seq model on a machine translation dataset, showing the model converging during training.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that the seq2seq model trains successfully, helping students connect the theoretical architecture to practical optimization behavior and set expectations for convergence.
when_to_show: Show after presenting the training loop code to demonstrate that the model learns; use to prompt discussion about loss curves and convergence criteria.
suggested_caption: Training loss curve for the RNN seq2seq model. (D2L.ai)

description: A training output visualization, likely a loss or metric curve from a different framework implementation (e.g., MXNet, JAX, or TensorFlow) of the same seq2seq training run, showing convergence behavior.
teaching_value: Allows comparison of training dynamics across different deep learning frameworks, reinforcing that the same algorithm produces consistent results regardless of implementation.
when_to_show: Show alongside other framework training outputs when discussing framework-agnostic implementation of seq2seq, or when students ask about reproducibility across frameworks.
suggested_caption: Seq2seq training loss curve (alternate framework). (D2L.ai)

description: Another training loss or metric curve from a third framework implementation of the seq2seq training experiment, showing the model's learning progress over training steps.
teaching_value: Further reinforces cross-framework consistency and helps students understand that the mathematical model is framework-independent, with minor differences in convergence speed or curve shape.
when_to_show: Show as part of a multi-framework comparison discussion, or when emphasizing the importance of framework-agnostic algorithm design.
suggested_caption: Seq2seq training loss (third framework implementation). (D2L.ai)

description: A fourth training loss or metric curve from yet another framework implementation of the seq2seq training, completing the set of multi-framework training result visualizations.
teaching_value: Completes the cross-framework comparison, giving students a full picture of how the same seq2seq model trains across all major frameworks covered in the textbook.
when_to_show: Show as the final entry in a side-by-side framework comparison, or when summarizing the training section to confirm consistent results.
suggested_caption: Seq2seq training loss (fourth framework implementation). (D2L.ai)

description: Diagram illustrating the seq2seq prediction (inference) process: the encoder processes the source sequence into a context vector, then the decoder generates output tokens autoregressively, feeding each predicted token as the next input until an EOS token is produced, without teacher forcing.
teaching_value: Highlights the key difference between training (teacher forcing) and inference (autoregressive prediction), showing students how the model generates translations token-by-token and how the EOS token terminates generation.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from the training section to the prediction/inference section, to contrast with the training diagram and explain why inference is more complex.
suggested_caption: Autoregressive seq2seq prediction: each output feeds the next step. (D2L.ai Fig. 10.7.3)

description: Photo of a Shiba Inu dog in a men's outfit with colored bounding boxes highlighting different regions — a yellow box on a pointy ear and red boxes on other facial features — illustrating selective visual attention.
teaching_value: Provides an intuitive, concrete analogy for how attention mechanisms work: focusing on high-resolution regions of interest while perceiving surroundings at lower resolution, directly motivating the mathematical formulation of attention.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of an attention lesson to build intuition before introducing any equations — ask students 'what are you looking at first and why?' to prime the concept.
suggested_caption: Visual attention: high-res focus on key regions, low-res periphery.

description: A sentence with attention weight visualizations showing how each word attends to other words in the same sentence, with varying highlight intensities indicating attention strength between word pairs.
teaching_value: Bridges the gap between visual attention and linguistic attention, showing concretely how a word's representation can depend on other words in the sentence — directly motivating self-attention in NLP models.
when_to_show: Show after the visual attention analogy and before introducing the mathematical definition of attention, to transition from images to text-based attention.
suggested_caption: Each word 'attends' to others with varying strength in a sentence.

description: Diagram of an encoder-decoder (seq2seq) architecture unrolled in time, showing an RNN encoder processing 'she is eating a green apple' into a fixed-length context vector, which a decoder then uses to generate the Chinese translation character by character.
teaching_value: Clearly illustrates the bottleneck problem of fixed-length context vectors in seq2seq models — the entire source sentence must be compressed into one vector — motivating why attention was invented.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the limitations of vanilla seq2seq models, before introducing attention — ask students 'what happens to early words when the sentence is very long?'
suggested_caption: Seq2seq encoder-decoder: source sentence compressed into one context vector.

description: Detailed architecture diagram of the Bahdanau additive attention mechanism in an encoder-decoder model, showing bidirectional encoder hidden states, alignment score computation, context vector as a weighted sum, and the decoder producing output tokens.
teaching_value: Shows exactly how attention resolves the bottleneck: instead of one fixed vector, the decoder dynamically computes a context vector as a weighted combination of all encoder hidden states at each decoding step, with the alignment weights visualized.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the seq2seq bottleneck problem; walk through each component step-by-step — encoder states, score function, softmax weights, weighted sum — before presenting the equations.
suggested_caption: Bahdanau additive attention: dynamic context from all encoder hidden states. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)

description: Heatmap alignment matrix where rows represent English output words and columns represent French input words; cell intensity shows the attention weight between each source-target word pair, revealing mostly diagonal alignment with some cross-language reordering.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that learned attention weights correspond to meaningful linguistic alignments, including non-monotonic reorderings between languages, validating the theoretical motivation for attention.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the attention weight formula to demonstrate that the model learns interpretable alignments — use the off-diagonal cells to discuss language reordering.
suggested_caption: Attention alignment matrix: French-to-English word correspondences learned automatically. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)

description: Visualization of self-attention in a machine reading task: the current word is highlighted in red and surrounding words are shaded in blue with varying intensity, showing which previous words the model attends to when processing the current word.
teaching_value: Demonstrates self-attention applied within a single sequence, showing that a word's representation is informed by contextually relevant earlier words — key to understanding intra-sequence dependencies.
when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention as distinct from encoder-decoder attention — highlight that both query and keys come from the same sequence.
suggested_caption: Self-attention: current word (red) attends to prior context (blue intensity). (Cheng et al., 2016)

description: Image captioning example showing a photo of a woman throwing a frisbee in a park, with attention weight overlays highlighting which image regions the model focuses on when generating each word of the caption.
teaching_value: Illustrates soft attention applied to image regions for caption generation, showing that attention weights form a soft probability distribution over spatial locations — contrasting with hard attention that selects a single patch.
when_to_show: Show when distinguishing soft vs. hard attention — use the overlay to explain that soft attention is differentiable (weighted sum) while hard attention would select exactly one region.
suggested_caption: Soft attention over image regions for caption generation. (Xu et al., 2015)

description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams comparing Luong's global attention (left, attending over all source positions) and local attention (right, attending over a window of source positions centered on a predicted alignment point), both showing the decoder hidden state, attention layer, and context vector computation.
teaching_value: Visually contrasts two attention variants — global (soft, full sequence) vs. local (hard-soft hybrid, windowed) — helping students understand the computational trade-off between coverage and efficiency.
when_to_show: Show after covering Bahdanau attention and soft/hard distinction — use the diagrams to compare architectures side by side and discuss when local attention is preferable for long sequences.
suggested_caption: Global vs. local attention: full sequence vs. windowed alignment. (Luong et al., 2015)

description: Architecture diagram of a Neural Turing Machine (NTM) showing the controller neural network connected to a memory matrix via read and write heads, with attention-based addressing mechanisms (content-based and location-based) producing attention weight vectors over memory rows.
teaching_value: Shows how attention mechanisms extend beyond sequence-to-sequence tasks to external memory access — the read/write heads use soft attention over memory locations, connecting NTM to the broader attention framework.
when_to_show: Show when extending attention concepts to memory-augmented networks — draw the parallel between NTM memory addressing and encoder-decoder attention weights explicitly.
suggested_caption: Neural Turing Machine: controller reads/writes memory via soft attention. (Graves et al., 2014)

description: Side-by-side comparison of two methods for representing the shift weighting distribution s_t in Neural Turing Machines: a softmax over shift positions and a direct parametric distribution.
teaching_value: Helps students understand the design choice in NTM location-based addressing — how the shift kernel is parameterized affects the model's ability to perform rotational shifts over memory locations.
when_to_show: When explaining NTM location-based addressing, specifically after introducing the interpolation gate and before discussing the sharpening step, to clarify how shift distributions are constructed.
suggested_caption: Two parameterizations of shift weighting distribution s_t in NTM addressing.

description: Full flow diagram of the NTM addressing mechanism showing the sequential steps: content-based addressing → interpolation with previous weights → location-based shift convolution → sharpening, producing the final attention vector w_t.
teaching_value: Provides a complete visual pipeline of how NTM combines content-based and location-based addressing, allowing students to trace how each component (key similarity, interpolation gate, shift kernel, sharpening scalar) contributes to the final memory attention vector.
when_to_show: Use as a summary diagram after introducing each individual NTM addressing component, or as an overview at the start of the NTM addressing section to give students a roadmap.
suggested_caption: NTM addressing pipeline: content lookup → interpolation → shift → sharpening. (Graves et al., 2014)

description: Architecture diagram of a Pointer Network showing an encoder RNN processing input sequence and a decoder RNN using attention over encoder states to output pointers (indices) back into the input sequence rather than fixed vocabulary tokens.
teaching_value: Illustrates how Pointer Networks differ from standard seq-2-seq models by using attention scores directly as output probabilities over input positions, enabling variable-length output vocabularies — key for understanding combinatorial optimization tasks like convex hull or TSP.
when_to_show: When introducing Pointer Networks, show this after explaining the limitation of standard seq-2-seq models with fixed output vocabularies, to visually demonstrate how attention is repurposed as a pointer.
suggested_caption: Pointer Network: decoder attention directly selects input positions as output. (Vinyals et al., 2015)

description: Diagram of multi-head scaled dot-product attention showing: (left) single scaled dot-product attention with Q, K, V inputs, softmax, and weighted sum; (right) multi-head attention with h parallel attention heads, linear projections, concatenation, and final linear layer.
teaching_value: Visually decomposes the multi-head attention mechanism into its components, helping students understand both the scaled dot-product operation and how multiple heads are run in parallel then merged — critical for understanding the Transformer architecture.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the Transformer's core attention mechanism; use the left panel first to explain single-head attention, then the right panel to explain parallelism and concatenation.
suggested_caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel heads and linear projection. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Diagram of the Transformer encoder stack showing: input embeddings with positional encoding, followed by N repeated layers each containing multi-head self-attention and a position-wise feed-forward network, both with residual connections and layer normalization.
teaching_value: Shows students the exact layer composition of the Transformer encoder, clarifying how positional encoding, self-attention, feed-forward sublayers, residual connections, and layer norm are stacked — essential for understanding how the encoder builds contextual representations.
when_to_show: After explaining multi-head attention in isolation, show this to demonstrate how it fits into the full encoder stack with residual connections and normalization.
suggested_caption: Transformer encoder: positional encoding, self-attention, and feed-forward layers stacked N times. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Diagram of the Transformer decoder stack showing: output embeddings with positional encoding, masked multi-head self-attention, encoder-decoder cross-attention, feed-forward network, residual connections, layer norm, and final linear+softmax output layer.
teaching_value: Illustrates how the decoder differs from the encoder by adding masked self-attention (to prevent future token leakage) and cross-attention over encoder outputs — helping students understand autoregressive generation and the role of each sublayer.
when_to_show: After explaining the encoder, show this to highlight the two key differences in the decoder: masking and cross-attention, and discuss why each is necessary for sequence generation.
suggested_caption: Transformer decoder with masked self-attention and encoder cross-attention layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Complete Transformer architecture diagram showing the full encoder (left) and decoder (right) stacks side by side, with data flow from input/output embeddings through all layers to the final softmax output probabilities.
teaching_value: Provides the holistic view of the entire Transformer model, allowing students to see how encoder and decoder interact, trace information flow end-to-end, and understand the complete architecture before or after studying individual components.
when_to_show: Use as an overview at the start of the Transformer section to orient students, and again as a synthesis diagram after covering encoder and decoder separately.
suggested_caption: Full Transformer architecture: encoder-decoder with multi-head attention and positional encoding. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: SNAIL (Simple Neural AttentIve Learner) architecture diagram showing alternating temporal convolutional layers (for dense context aggregation) and causal attention layers (for pinpointing specific past information) used for meta-learning tasks.
teaching_value: Demonstrates how attention mechanisms can be combined with causal convolutions to handle sequential dependency in meta-learning, showing students a practical hybrid architecture that addresses the Transformer's weak positional encoding for RL-sensitive tasks.
when_to_show: When discussing limitations of the Transformer's positional encoding for reinforcement learning or meta-learning, show this as an example of a hybrid architecture that compensates with explicit temporal convolutions.
suggested_caption: SNAIL combines causal convolutions and attention for meta-learning tasks. (Mishra et al., 2017)

description: Side-by-side visual comparison showing the limited local receptive field of a convolutional operation (small highlighted region) versus the global receptive field of self-attention (entire image accessible), illustrating the spatial coverage difference.
teaching_value: Gives students an intuitive visual understanding of why self-attention is more powerful than convolution for capturing long-range spatial dependencies in images — motivating the use of self-attention in GANs.
when_to_show: When introducing SAGAN or comparing CNNs to attention-based models, show this early to build intuition for why convolutions struggle with global context before explaining the self-attention solution.
suggested_caption: Convolution has local receptive field; self-attention accesses all spatial positions globally.

description: Diagram of the self-attention mechanism in SAGAN showing convolutional feature maps branched into three paths (f, g, h corresponding to key, query, value), attention map computation via softmax, weighted sum to produce attended features, and residual addition to input.
teaching_value: Concretely shows how the key-query-value attention framework from Transformers is adapted to 2D image feature maps using 1x1 convolutions, helping students transfer their understanding of NLP attention to the vision/GAN domain.
when_to_show: After explaining the Transformer's attention mechanism, show this to demonstrate how the same KQV framework applies to image feature maps, reinforcing the generality of the attention concept across domains.
suggested_caption: SAGAN self-attention: image features split into key, query, value via 1×1 convolutions. (Zhang et al., 2018)

description: A grid of 128×128 generated images from the Self-Attention GAN (SAGAN) model, showing diverse object classes such as dogs, birds, and other animals, demonstrating the visual quality and diversity of outputs achievable with self-attention mechanisms in generative adversarial networks.
teaching_value: Provides concrete visual evidence of what self-attention mechanisms enable in image generation — students can see that SAGAN produces globally coherent, high-fidelity images by allowing the model to attend to distant spatial locations, motivating why self-attention is beneficial over purely local convolutional operations.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the SAGAN attention mechanism and the attention map formula, to give students a tangible payoff — use it to ask: 'What visual properties (e.g., fur texture consistency, symmetry) might require long-range attention to generate correctly?'
suggested_caption: SAGAN-generated 128×128 images showcasing self-attention quality. (Zhang et al., 2018)

description: A classic RNN encoder–decoder (seq2seq) diagram: source tokens are encoded into a fixed-dimensional context/state, which is then passed to an RNN decoder that generates target tokens step by step.
teaching_value: Clarifies the fixed-length bottleneck in vanilla seq2seq: all source information must be compressed into one context vector, motivating attention.
when_to_show: Show right before introducing attention, when students ask why long sentences degrade translation quality.
suggested_caption: Vanilla seq2seq uses a single context vector for decoding (Dive into Deep Learning).

description: A detailed seq2seq-with-attention architecture: encoder hidden states across all time steps feed an additive (Bahdanau) attention module; at each decoder step, the previous decoder hidden state forms the query, producing a context vector that is combined with the current input embedding to update the decoder state and predict the next token.
teaching_value: Shows the alignment/attention loop concretely—how queries, keys/values, attention weights, and the context vector integrate into the RNN decoder at every time step.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how attention replaces a single fixed context with a time-varying context per output token.
suggested_caption: Bahdanau attention in an RNN encoder–decoder (Dive into Deep Learning).
